threats.title: PermissionValidator Out-of-Bounds Calldata Read Enables Session Key Permission Bypass
threats.hypothesis_description: LibBytes.readBytes32 does not perform bounds checking on calldata slices. In PermissionValidator.validatePermission, when rule.offset exceeds call.data.length, calldataload reads from adjacent ABI-encoded data in the transaction calldata (other payload fields, signature data) rather than the actual call.data sent to the target. Since the session key holder controls the full transaction payload, they can arrange ABI-encoded data at specific offsets to satisfy permission rules, while the target contract only receives the shorter call.data with different or zero-padded parameters.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental mismatch between what the permission validator checks and what the target contract receives. The attacker (session key holder) can craft payloads where: (1) call.data is shorter than rule.offset, causing OOB read, (2) Other attacker-controlled data in the calldata satisfies the rule, (3) The target sees truncated/different arguments. This enables exceeding permission boundaries without any off-chain coordination.

proof_of_signal.pattern: readBytes32 uses calldataload(add(_pointer, _data.offset)) without checking _pointer < _data.length; returns whatever is at that raw calldata position

preconditions: Wallet has active session key with explicit permissions using parameter rules
Permission rules check calldata at specific offsets
Session key holder crafts payload with call.data shorter than rule.offset

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: (bytes32 value,) = call.data.readBytes32(rule.offset) where rule.offset can exceed call.data.length
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Session key sends short call.data; PermissionValidator reads from adjacent ABI-encoded payload data that the attacker controls; target contract receives only the short call.data
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Session key bypasses permission constraints to execute unauthorized transfers or function calls up to the session value limit

economic_questions: Can the attacker reliably predict/control ABI-encoded data at specific offsets after call.data?
What types of permission rules are most susceptible (EQUAL checks on specific values)?
Can this bypass enable calls that drain wallet assets beyond session bounds?
validation_requirements: Construct a PoC where call.data is 4 bytes but permission rule reads at offset 36, satisfied by attacker-controlled payload data
Verify the target contract receives only the 4-byte call.data
Measure impact: unauthorized token transfers, unauthorized function calls

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to entire wallet balance for session's value limit
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Near 100% of extracted value minus gas

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time the session key is active
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Direct theft from wallet, no market impact needed

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Wallet owner who granted session key with restricted permissions
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to full wallet balance within session value limits
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All assets in wallets using explicit session permissions with parameter rules

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: ABI encoding layout of Payload.Decoded calldata in SessionManager.recoverSapientSignature
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace exact calldataload offset when rule.offset > call.data.length

threats.title: Unchecked Weight Overflow in BaseSig.recoverBranch Causes DoS for Subdigest Configurations
threats.hypothesis_description: BaseSig.recoverBranch operates entirely within an unchecked block. When FLAG_SUBDIGEST matches, weight is set to type(uint256).max. If any subsequent signer in the same branch adds weight (weight += addrWeight), the uint256 overflows to a small value (e.g., max + 1 = 0). The threshold check (weight >= threshold) then fails, causing the signature to be rejected even though the subdigest should grant unconditional access.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a concrete code defect in arithmetic. Any wallet configuration that includes a subdigest leaf alongside other signers in the same Merkle branch cannot authenticate using the subdigest path. An attacker who knows the wallet config can force signature rejection by including the subdigest match, effectively locking the wallet out of subdigest-based operations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Entire recoverBranch is in unchecked{}; FLAG_SUBDIGEST sets weight=type(uint256).max at line ~260; subsequent FLAG_SIGNATURE_HASH does weight+=addrWeight at line ~175 causing overflow

preconditions: Wallet configuration has a subdigest leaf AND other signer leaves in the same Merkle branch
The subdigest matches the current operation hash
At least one additional signer contributes weight in the same recoverBranch call

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: weight = type(uint256).max; then weight += addrWeight; in unchecked block → weight wraps to addrWeight - 1
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Provide a valid signature including matched subdigest + any additional signer in same branch
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: DoS: legitimate wallet operations that rely on subdigest authentication are permanently blocked for affected configurations

economic_questions: How many production wallets use subdigest leaves with other signers in the same branch?
Can this be exploited to permanently lock wallet funds?
Is there a workaround (restructuring the config tree)?
validation_requirements: Create a wallet config with subdigest + signer in same branch, verify signature rejection
Test with subdigest in sub-branch (FLAG_BRANCH) vs same-level branch
Verify parent branch weight aggregation also overflows

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: locked funds in affected wallets

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Affects wallet configuration permanently once deployed
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Wallet funds inaccessible via subdigest path

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Wallets with subdigest + other signers in same branch
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of subdigest authentication path; may lock funds if no alternative auth path exists
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All funds in affected wallet configurations

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Test weight=max + 1, weight=max + 2, etc. in unchecked context
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify overflow propagation through nested recoverBranch calls

threats.title: Guest Contract Allows Unauthorized ETH Drain by Any Caller
threats.hypothesis_description: The Guest contract's fallback function decodes and executes arbitrary calls from msg.data without any authentication, signature validation, or nonce protection. Any ETH sent to the Guest contract (accidentally or intentionally) can be immediately drained by anyone by calling the Guest with a packed payload that transfers the ETH balance to the attacker.
threats.investigation_rationale: Guest is deployed as a singleton (see Deploy.s.sol). If any user or contract accidentally sends ETH to the Guest address (e.g., wrong address, misconfigured integration), anyone can drain it. The Guest has a receive() function (inherited from Hooks? No - Guest doesn't inherit Hooks. But it has a fallback that's payable implicitly via Payload execution). Actually, the fallback itself is `payable` and LibOptim.call forwards value. If ETH arrives at Guest via any means, any caller can execute arbitrary calls including value transfers.
threats.config_evidence: Deploy.s.sol deploys Guest as a singleton contract at a deterministic address

proof_of_signal.pattern: fallback() external payable decodes msg.data as packed calls and executes them without any authentication or signature check

preconditions: Guest contract holds ETH (sent accidentally or through misconfigured interaction)
Attacker knows the Guest contract address

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No authentication: anyone can call Guest fallback with arbitrary packed payload
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Craft packed payload with call to attacker address transferring full Guest ETH balance
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: 100% of ETH held by Guest contract

economic_questions: Is Guest expected to hold ETH in any legitimate workflow?
Can the Guest receive ETH through normal fallback (no explicit receive)?
Are there integrations that might mistakenly send ETH to Guest?
validation_requirements: Send ETH to Guest, then call fallback with drain payload
Verify no access controls exist
Check if Guest contract can receive ETH via direct transfer

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All ETH held by Guest contract

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after ETH is sent to Guest

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Anyone who accidentally sends ETH to the Guest contract
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Any ETH sent to Guest address

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check Guest's ability to receive ETH (payable fallback)
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify packed payload can encode ETH transfer to arbitrary address

threats.title: ecrecover Returning address(0) Grants Weight to Unchecked Signer in BaseSig
threats.hypothesis_description: In BaseSig.recoverBranch, FLAG_SIGNATURE_HASH and FLAG_SIGNATURE_ETH_SIGN recover a signer address via ecrecover. When ecrecover fails (invalid signature), it returns address(0). The code unconditionally adds the addrWeight to the total weight and computes a leaf for address(0). If a wallet configuration includes address(0) as a signer with non-zero weight, any invalid compact signature (e.g., r=0, s=0) will satisfy that signer slot, granting its weight without any valid private key.
threats.investigation_rationale: While including address(0) as a signer is unusual, it represents a code defect: the signature recovery should validate that the recovered address is non-zero before granting weight. If any configuration tooling bug or manual misconfiguration includes address(0), the wallet becomes trivially compromisable.
deployment_assumptions: Wallet configuration includes address(0) as a signer with non-zero weight
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence of address(0) signers; requires misconfiguration or tooling bug

proof_of_signal.pattern: FLAG_SIGNATURE_HASH: ecrecover result used directly without address(0) check; weight += addrWeight unconditionally; leaf computed for potentially address(0)

preconditions: Wallet config includes address(0) as a signer with weight >= threshold
OR address(0) has sufficient weight to meet threshold when combined with other signers

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: address addr = ecrecover(_opHash, v, r, s); weight += addrWeight; // no check addr != address(0)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Submit any invalid ECDSA signature (e.g., r=0,s=0) to trigger ecrecover returning address(0)
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Full wallet takeover if address(0) signer weight meets threshold

economic_questions: Do any production SDK paths allow creating configs with address(0) signers?
Could address(0) appear through a Merkle tree construction error?
What is the maximum weight an address(0) signer could have?
validation_requirements: Create wallet config with address(0) signer, submit invalid signature, verify weight granted
Test edge cases in config generation that could produce address(0) entries

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after misconfigured wallet is deployed

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Wallet owners with misconfigured address(0) signers

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Review SDK/tooling for address(0) signer generation paths

threats.title: Recovery Payloads Cannot Be Cancelled, Creating Irreversible Time-Bomb After Key Compromise
threats.hypothesis_description: Recovery.queuePayload stores payload hashes with their queuing timestamp, but provides no mechanism to cancel queued payloads. Once a recovery signer's key is compromised and used to queue a malicious config update, the payload becomes permanently pending. The wallet owner must update the wallet configuration to invalidate the recovery signer before the timelock expires. If the owner fails to act within the requiredDeltaTime window, the attacker can execute the recovery and take over the wallet.
threats.investigation_rationale: The write-once nature of timestampForQueuedPayload (checked via AlreadyQueued) and the absence of any cancellation function create a permanent attack surface. The queuedPayloadHashes array also grows unboundedly, and there's no garbage collection. The requiredDeltaTime is stored as uint24 (max ~194 days), limiting the protection window.

proof_of_signal.pattern: queuePayload stores timestamp permanently; no cancel/remove function exists; AlreadyQueued prevents re-queuing but not cancellation

preconditions: Wallet uses Recovery module as a sapient signer
Recovery signer's private key is compromised
Attacker queues a malicious config update payload
Wallet owner does not update config before timelock expires

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: timestampForQueuedPayload set once, never cleared; no cancel function
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Queue malicious payload, wait for timelock, execute recovery to take over wallet
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Full wallet takeover after timelock expiry if owner doesn't respond

economic_questions: What is the typical requiredDeltaTime for production recovery configs?
Can the wallet owner reliably detect and respond to queued payloads?
Is the unbounded queuedPayloadHashes array a DoS vector?
validation_requirements: Verify no cancellation path exists in Recovery.sol
Test that config update before timelock invalidates the recovery
Assess gas cost of unbounded array growth

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All wallet assets if owner doesn't respond in time

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When wallet owner is likely unavailable (holidays, weekends)

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Wallet owners with compromised recovery signers
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All assets in wallets using Recovery module

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify wallet config update invalidates queued payloads

threats.title: LibBytes Unchecked Reads Enable Silent Signature/Payload Misparse Across Multiple Contexts
threats.hypothesis_description: LibBytes functions (readUint8, readAddress, readBytes32, etc.) use raw calldataload without bounds checking. When reading beyond the actual data slice, calldataload returns zero-padded values. This affects multiple contexts: (1) BaseSig.recoverBranch parsing truncated signatures may produce zero addresses/weights, (2) Payload.fromPackedCalls parsing truncated payloads may produce calls to address(0) with zero values, (3) SessionSig.recoverConfiguration parsing may produce malformed configurations. Any of these could lead to unexpected behavior.
threats.investigation_rationale: The absence of bounds checking is documented in LibBytes but creates systemic risk across all consumers. A truncated signature could be parsed with zero-filled values that coincidentally match a valid configuration element. The impact depends on downstream validation catching these zero values, which is inconsistent across different code paths.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Documentation states: 'These functions do not check if the input index is within the bounds of the data array. Reading out of bounds may return dirty values.'

preconditions: Attacker crafts a truncated signature or payload
Zero-filled values from OOB reads produce valid-looking data downstream
Downstream checks don't catch all zero-value edge cases

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: calldataload beyond slice returns 0; parsed as address(0), weight 0, size 0, etc.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Submit truncated signatures that parse with zero-padded values to bypass validation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Potential signature forgery or payload manipulation depending on config

economic_questions: Which code paths are most vulnerable to zero-filled OOB reads?
Can a truncated signature produce a valid imageHash?
Can a truncated payload produce exploitable calls to address(0)?
validation_requirements: Fuzz BaseSig.recover with truncated signatures, check for unexpected valid parses
Test Payload.fromPackedCalls with truncated inputs
Map all LibBytes consumers and their zero-value handling

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Varies - could be full wallet compromise in worst case

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Map all calldataload sites vs data length checks
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Systematic testing of truncated inputs across all parsers

threats.title: SessionSig Blacklist Allows Duplicate Entries Due to Non-Strict Sort Check
threats.hypothesis_description: SessionSig.recoverConfiguration checks blacklist sorting with strict less-than (<) instead of less-than-or-equal (<=), allowing duplicate consecutive addresses. While duplicates don't break the binary search, they affect the imageHash computation (same config with/without duplicates produces different imageHashes). More critically, address(0) can be the first entry since previousAddress starts as address(0) and the check is `sig.implicitBlacklist[i] < previousAddress` (address(0) < address(0) is false, so it passes). This could lead to a mismatch between intended and actual blacklist behavior.
threats.investigation_rationale: The blacklist is critical for implicit session security - it prevents implicit sessions from calling dangerous contracts. A misconfigured blacklist (with duplicates or address(0)) could lead to incorrect imageHash computation or unexpected blacklist behavior. The binary search correctly handles duplicates, but the imageHash includes the raw encoded blacklist, so duplicate-containing and deduplicated configs produce different hashes.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (sig.implicitBlacklist[i] < previousAddress) revert; uses < not <= allowing equal consecutive addresses

preconditions: Session configuration includes blacklist with duplicate addresses
OR session configuration includes address(0) in blacklist

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Duplicate addresses allowed: address < address is false, doesn't revert
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Construct session config with duplicate blacklist entries to create unexpected imageHash
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Config mismatch could lead to blacklist bypass in specific scenarios

economic_questions: Can duplicate blacklist entries cause config verification failures?
Does address(0) in blacklist have any security implications?
Can an attacker exploit the imageHash difference between duplicate/non-duplicate configs?
validation_requirements: Test blacklist with duplicate entries for binary search correctness
Verify imageHash computation with duplicates vs without
Test address(0) blacklist entry behavior

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Potential indirect theft via implicit session abuse

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Test binary search with duplicates and edge values
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify sort check prevents all unsorted inputs

threats.title: Nonce Unchecked Overflow Allows Theoretical Replay After type(uint256).max Transactions
threats.hypothesis_description: Nonce._consumeNonce uses an unchecked block for nonce increment: `unchecked { uint256 newNonce = _nonce + 1; }`. At type(uint256).max, the nonce wraps to 0, enabling replay of every previously executed transaction in that nonce space. While reaching this limit requires 2^256 transactions (computationally impossible), the code defect violates the stated invariant of strict sequential nonce advancement.
threats.investigation_rationale: The nonce overflow violates INV-STATE-003 (nonce must strictly increment) and INV-ECON-003 (nonce conservation). Though impractical, this represents a correctness defect. A simple `require(_nonce < type(uint256).max)` check would eliminate the theoretical risk at negligible gas cost.

proof_of_signal.pattern: unchecked { uint256 newNonce = _nonce + 1; } wraps at max uint256

preconditions: Nonce in a specific space reaches type(uint256).max (computationally impossible)
Attacker has stored valid signed transactions from nonce 0 onwards

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: uint256 newNonce = _nonce + 1 in unchecked context; at max wraps to 0
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After overflow, replay any historical signed transaction
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Theoretical replay of all historical transactions

economic_questions: Is 2^256 transactions achievable within any conceivable timeframe?
Could a future chain have different nonce semantics that reduce this number?
validation_requirements: Verify unchecked overflow behavior at type(uint256).max
Confirm no other overflow protection exists

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify unchecked addition wraps at max
